title: AED (Automated External Defibrillator) Operation - Child and Infant Protocol

source.document_title: AED Operation for Children and Infants - MDA Defibrillator Guidance (based on AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC)
source.edition: AHA 2025 Guidelines as adopted by MDA; corroborated by Hebrew-language media sources (Ynet, Maariv) and Israeli AED legislation (aed.org.il)

import_metadata.notes: Unified from MDA and United Hatzalah sources. MDA used as authoritative default. No substantive differences found between the two organisations -- both follow AHA-based guidelines for paediatric AED use. United Hatzalah does not publish a distinct paediatric AED protocol; their public guidance confirms they follow the same AHA-based guidelines as MDA. MDA does not publish a standalone English-language AED protocol; this protocol is synthesised from MDA's defibrillator information page, Hebrew-language media articles based on MDA guidance, and AHA 2025 guidelines that MDA formally adopts. Age categories per MDA/AHA: infant (under 1 year), child (1-8 years). Children aged 8 and above or showing signs of puberty should use the adult AED protocol.

protocol.summary: Use an AED on a child or infant in cardiac arrest: activate paediatric mode or use paediatric pads if available, apply anteroposterior pad placement for small chests, follow device prompts, and integrate with age-appropriate CPR.
protocol.when_to_apply: Child (age 1-8) or infant (under 1 year) who is unresponsive, not breathing normally (no breathing or only gasping/agonal breathing), and in suspected cardiac arrest (no pulse if trained to check).

call_emergency.when: If a bystander is present, send them to call 101 (MDA) and fetch the nearest AED while you begin CPR. If alone with a child, perform 2 minutes of CPR first (cardiac arrest in children is more often respiratory in origin), then call 101 and retrieve an AED.

steps.instruction: Recognise cardiac arrest in the child or infant.
steps.detail: Check responsiveness by calling out and tapping or shaking the child's shoulders (for infants, flick the sole of the foot). Check breathing for no more than 10 seconds -- look, listen, and feel. If the child is unresponsive and not breathing normally (absent breathing or only gasping), suspect cardiac arrest.

steps.instruction: Call for help and send for an AED.
steps.detail: If a bystander is present, instruct them to call 101 (MDA) immediately and retrieve the nearest AED. Use the Eifo-Defi app to locate the nearest device, or call 101 -- MDA dispatch can locate the nearest smart stand and remotely unlock it. Check nearby buildings, shopping centres, train stations, and sports facilities.

decision_point.condition: Is a bystander available to help?
decision_point.if_yes: Send the bystander to call 101 and fetch the nearest AED. Begin CPR immediately (proceed to Step 3).
decision_point.if_no: If alone, perform 2 minutes of CPR first (approximately 5 cycles of 30:2), then call 101 and retrieve the AED. Cardiac arrest in children is more often respiratory in origin, so early CPR is critical.

steps.instruction: Begin CPR immediately while waiting for the AED.
steps.detail: Place the child on a firm, flat surface. Begin chest compressions appropriate to the child's age. For children (1-8 years): use the heel of one hand on the centre of the chest, compress to approximately one-third of chest depth (about 5 cm / 2 inches), at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute. For infants (under 1 year): use two fingers on the sternum just below the nipple line (or two-thumb encircling technique with two rescuers), compress to approximately one-third of chest depth (about 4 cm / 1.5 inches). Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions (solo rescuer) or 15 compressions (two trained rescuers).

decision_point.condition: Is the victim an infant (under 1 year)?
decision_point.if_yes: Use two-finger compression technique (solo rescuer) or two-thumb encircling technique (two rescuers). Compress to about 4 cm / 1.5 inches. Use slight head tilt only when opening the airway for rescue breaths.
decision_point.if_no: Use one-hand compression technique on the centre of the chest. Compress to about 5 cm / 2 inches. Use standard head-tilt chin-lift for rescue breaths.

steps.instruction: When the AED arrives, open and power on the device.
steps.detail: Open the AED cover -- most devices power on automatically when opened (some models require pressing a power button). The AED will begin providing voice instructions in Hebrew (and other languages depending on the model). Put MDA dispatch on speakerphone if still on the call -- they will guide you.

steps.instruction: Activate paediatric mode on the AED.
steps.detail: Many AEDs in Israel have a paediatric mode switch -- a button or key that switches the device to deliver a lower energy dose appropriate for children. Activate this before applying pads if the patient is a child under 8 years or under 25 kg. MDA notes that 'the device has a switch to child mode and/or pad replacement depending on the model.'

decision_point.condition: Does the AED have a paediatric mode switch or paediatric pads available?
decision_point.if_yes: Activate the paediatric mode switch and/or use the paediatric pads (reduced energy dose). Proceed to Step 6.
decision_point.if_no: Use adult pads -- do NOT delay defibrillation because paediatric pads are unavailable. The AED can still be used safely. Proceed to Step 6.

steps.warning: Do NOT delay defibrillation to search for paediatric pads or a paediatric mode. If unavailable, use adult pads immediately.

steps.instruction: Expose the child's bare chest and prepare the skin.
steps.detail: Remove or cut away clothing. Ensure the chest is dry -- wipe off sweat, water, or moisture. Remove any medication patches from the chest area and wipe the skin. If there is an implanted pacemaker/defibrillator (visible lump under the skin), place pads at least 2.5 cm (1 inch) away from the device.

steps.instruction: Apply the electrode pads using the correct placement for the child's size.
steps.detail: Follow the diagram printed on the pads. For small children and infants, anteroposterior (front-and-back) placement is preferred because the chest may be too small for both pads to fit on the front without touching.

decision_point.condition: Is the child's chest large enough for both pads to be placed on the front (anterolateral) with at least 2.5 cm between them and no overlap?
decision_point.if_yes: Use anterolateral placement (same as adults): Pad 1 below the right collarbone to the right of the sternum; Pad 2 on the left side of the chest below the armpit in line with the nipple.
decision_point.if_no: Use anteroposterior (front-and-back) placement: Front pad on the centre of the chest (sternum) at the level of the heart; Back pad on the centre of the back between the shoulder blades, directly behind the front pad.

steps.warning: Pads must NEVER touch or overlap each other. If the child's chest is too small for anterolateral placement, always use anteroposterior placement.

steps.instruction: Connect the pads to the AED if not pre-connected.
steps.detail: Plug the pad cable into the AED connector if the pads are not already pre-connected. Ensure the pads are firmly adhered to the skin with no air gaps.

steps.instruction: Allow the AED to analyse the heart rhythm.
steps.detail: The AED will instruct: 'Do not touch the patient' / 'Analysing heart rhythm.' Stop CPR and ensure nobody is touching the child during analysis. The AED will determine whether a shockable rhythm (ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia) is present.
steps.warning: Everyone must stop touching the child during rhythm analysis. Any movement or contact can interfere with the analysis.

decision_point.condition: Does the AED advise a shock ('Shock advised')?
decision_point.if_yes: Ensure nobody is touching the child. Call out clearly: 'Stand clear!' (Hebrew: 'Hitrachku!'). Press the shock button when instructed. Some fully automatic AEDs deliver the shock without pressing a button. After the shock, immediately resume CPR (proceed to Step 11).
decision_point.if_no: The AED says 'No shock advised.' Resume CPR immediately (proceed to Step 11). The AED will not deliver a shock if the rhythm is not shockable -- the device cannot harm the child.

steps.warning: Ensure all bystanders are clear of the child before delivering the shock. Visually verify nobody is touching the child.

steps.instruction: Immediately resume CPR after the shock or 'no shock' advisory.
steps.detail: Continue age-appropriate CPR for 2 minutes (approximately 5 cycles of 30:2 for a solo rescuer, or 15:2 for two trained rescuers). Use the correct compression technique for the child's age: one-hand for children 1-8 years, two-finger or two-thumb for infants. The AED will automatically re-analyse the rhythm after 2 minutes.

steps.instruction: Continue the cycle of CPR and AED analysis until help arrives.
steps.detail: Repeat the cycle: CPR for 2 minutes, then AED analysis, then shock if advised, then CPR again. Do NOT turn off the AED. Leave pads attached at all times, even if the child shows signs of recovery.

decision_point.condition: Has the child shown obvious signs of life (moves, breathes normally, opens eyes) OR have MDA paramedics arrived?
decision_point.if_yes: If paramedics arrive, hand over to them. If the child shows signs of life, stop compressions but leave AED pads attached and the device on -- monitor closely and be ready to resume CPR if the child deteriorates. Place in recovery position if breathing normally.
decision_point.if_no: Continue the CPR and AED cycle. Do not stop unless MDA paramedics take over, the child shows obvious signs of life, or you are physically unable to continue.

do_not: DO NOT use an AED on a child who is conscious and breathing normally -- AEDs are only for cardiac arrest.
DO NOT use an AED while the child is submerged in water or lying in a puddle -- move them to dry ground first before applying pads.
DO NOT use an AED near flammable gases or oxygen-enriched atmospheres.
DO NOT delay defibrillation because paediatric pads are unavailable -- use adult pads with anteroposterior placement if needed.
DO NOT allow pads to touch or overlap each other -- use anteroposterior placement if the chest is too small for anterolateral placement.
DO NOT touch the child during AED rhythm analysis or shock delivery.
DO NOT turn off the AED or remove the pads once applied, even if the child regains consciousness.
DO NOT use an AED on a child who is breathing and has a pulse -- place in recovery position and monitor instead.
DO NOT perform adult-depth compressions on a child or infant -- compress to one-third of chest depth only.
DO NOT use adult pads with standard anterolateral placement on an infant or small child if the pads would touch or overlap -- switch to anteroposterior placement.
equipment_needed: Paediatric AED pads (preferred for children under 8 / under 25 kg)
Adult AED pads (if paediatric pads unavailable)
Scissors or shears (to cut away clothing)
Towel or cloth (to dry the chest)
Razor (included in many AED kits, for removing excessive chest hair if needed)
